summary:
The Vakataka Dynasty (c. 250–500 AD) was a powerful Indian dynasty that ruled the Deccan plateau (central India) during the same era as the [[event:83|Gupta Empire]] to the north, forming a crucial alliance through marriage that helped stabilize both dynasties and enabled the remarkable cultural achievements of the Indian Golden Age. The Vakatakas controlled a strategic territory between the northern Gupta domains and the Dravidian south, and their alliance with the Guptas — cemented when Gupta emperor Chandragupta II gave his daughter Prabhavati Gupta in marriage to the Vakataka king Rudrasena II — effectively created a pan-Indian axis of power in the 4th–5th centuries.

The Vakatakas are most celebrated for their patronage of the [[event:2057|Ajanta cave temples]] in their territory: the great second phase of Ajanta painting (the Mahayana Buddhist murals) was commissioned under Vakataka royal patronage, making them directly responsible for some of the greatest artworks of the ancient world. They were also patrons of Sanskrit literature, with court poets producing important works in the Gupta literary tradition. The dynasty declined in the late 5th century after a series of defeats and succession crises, fragmenting into smaller successor states as the Hephthalite invasions simultaneously undermined Gupta power to the north. The Vakatakas represent an important and somewhat underappreciated dimension of the Indian classical period, demonstrating that the Golden Age extended well beyond the Gupta heartland.
